findings.description: json.loads() is called on LLM-returned strings (raw_analysis) and user-provided input is compared against hardcoded string literals. No path for user input to reach json.loads(). Risk is low — only LLM output is parsed.
findings.remediation: None required. json.loads() result is used internally and errors are caught by JSONDecodeError handler.

findings.description: Line 45 uses 'yield from' in an async function _select_strategy_group(). This is a Python syntax error — yield from is not valid in async def. This function appears dead/unreachable as the main flow in _run_two_phase_l2() (lines 177-193) handles strategy group selection inline instead.
findings.remediation: Remove or fix the unused _select_strategy_group() function. The duplicate logic in _run_two_phase_l2() lines 177-193 is the active code path.

summary: No command injection, SQL injection, path traversal, or hardcoded secrets. One low-severity finding (json.loads on LLM output, properly error-handled). One informational note about a dead/unreachable function with a syntax error. No exploitable vulnerabilities for an attacker with CLI access.

prompt_injection.note: User input only used for string comparison, never embedded in LLM prompts
